medications.indication_rationale: Oral tetracycline for moderate-to-severe inflammatory acne — anti-inflammatory and anti-C. acnes effect. Prescriber to confirm dosing for age/weight.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 100 mg orally daily (as per source note — confirm at prescriber review)
contraindications_in_this_case: Avoid in children <8 years and in pregnancy — confirm age; take with water, remain upright 30 min, photosensitivity advice

medications.indication_rationale: Topical antibiotic to reduce inflammatory lesions; ideally combined with benzoyl peroxide to limit resistance.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply topically to affected areas (as per source note)

medications.indication_rationale: Simple analgesia for the painful flare, taken with food PRN. Paracetamol is an alternative.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 400 mg orally with food PRN (as per source note — confirm for age)

medications.drug_name: intravenous / systemic antibiotics for presumed facial cellulitis
medications.indication_rationale: There is no systemic toxicity or spreading demarcated cellulitis; treating empirically as cellulitis (IV antibiotics, ED referral) is an error of commission driven by the triage framing.
Not first-line for a routine acne flare in primary care; short intralesional/oral steroid is a specialist decision, not the initial telehealth action.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply a warm, damp compress to the painful cysts for ~10 minutes each evening to ease pressure and encourage drainage; do not squeeze or pick the lesions.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces pain and scarring risk; picking worsens inflammation and scarring.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Take the antibiotic daily with a full glass of water, staying upright for 30 minutes.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Prevents oesophageal irritation and improves adherence.

patient_education_points.point: This looks like a severe flare of your acne rather than a spreading skin infection — but because the phone nurse was worried about infection, we watch closely for the warning signs below.
The oral antibiotic lowers the deep inflammation and bacteria; the acute pain should ease over 48–72 hours, but the acne itself takes weeks to months of consistent treatment.
Do not squeeze the cysts — it worsens pain and scarring. Use the warm compresses instead.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Antibiotic response, pain, lesion resolution; plan longer-term acne management and consider dermatology referral for scarring/severe disease.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: If redness spreads toward the eyes, vision changes, fever >38.5°C or chills develop, or pain becomes unmanageable — seek emergency care immediately.

must_include_items: Identify severe inflammatory acne flare as the working diagnosis while explicitly screening for/ excluding facial and periorbital cellulitis
Arrange urgent same-day in-person clinician review for assessment and prescribing
Oral tetracycline (e.g., doxycycline) plus topical therapy, with dosing confirmed at prescriber review
Explicit safety-net for periorbital spread/visual change/high fever/rigors
acceptable_alternatives: Paracetamol instead of ibuprofen for analgesia
Combining topical clindamycin with benzoyl peroxide
Alternative oral tetracycline (e.g., minocycline) where appropriate
errors_of_omission: No safety-net advice for eye involvement or systemic features
errors_of_commission: Diagnosing facial cellulitis and sending to ED for IV antibiotics without red flags (over-triage)
Ordering blood cultures/CT for presumed sepsis
Advising the patient to squeeze/incise the cysts themselves
Invented paediatric antibiotic dose without clinician verification
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires BOTH prompt acne-directed management AND an explicit periorbital/cellulitis safety-net. Over-triage to ED/IV antibiotics without red flags, or dismissive reassurance without a safety-net, are the key failure modes.
